question: [File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/TransactionRepository.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findByConsensusTimestampBetween(long startInclusive, long endInclusive, Pageable pageable)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the nonce field in the Transaction entity to craft a Pageable sort by nonce that reorders transactions with the same consensusTimestamp, making child/scheduled transactions appear in a different sequence than their actual consensus order?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/TransactionRepository.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: prune(long consensusTimestamp)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that prune() deletes from the Transaction entity (mapped to a partitioned table) without explicitly specifying a partition, causing the DELETE to acquire table-level locks on all partitions and blocking all concurrent history reads, effectively making history temporarily unavailable (reorganized to empty)?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/TransactionRepository.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findByConsensusTimestampBetween(long startInclusive, long endInclusive, Pageable pageable)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the index field in the Transaction entity (transaction index within a block) to sort results by index via Pageable, causing transactions from different blocks to be interleaved in the result set and misrepresenting the true block-level transaction ordering?
